questions.stem: Which ECG finding most strongly suggests atrial fibrillation?
options: Regular rhythm with visible P waves before every QRS
Irregularly irregular rhythm without clear P waves
ST elevation in contiguous leads only
Short PR interval with a delta wave
questions.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is caused by disorganized atrial activity, so the ECG classically shows no consistent P waves and an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm.
wrong: Regular rhythm with P waves suggests sinus rhythm, not atrial fibrillation.
ST elevation in contiguous leads suggests acute myocardial infarction rather than atrial fibrillation.
Short PR with a delta wave suggests Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern.
Tall peaked T waves suggest hyperkalemia.
questions.takeaway: AF = irregularly irregular rhythm + absent organized P waves.

questions.stem: A narrow-complex tachycardia that terminates after carotid sinus massage is most consistent with which mechanism?
questions.explanation: AVNRT is an AV-node dependent supraventricular tachycardia. Vagal maneuvers may increase AV nodal refractoriness and terminate the rhythm.
wrong: Ventricular fibrillation is chaotic, pulseless, and not terminated by carotid massage.
Complete heart block causes bradycardia rather than narrow-complex tachycardia.
Atrial flutter may slow with AV nodal blockade but usually does not terminate with carotid massage.
questions.takeaway: Regular narrow tachycardia stopped by vagal maneuver = AVNRT.

questions.stem: Which rhythm is most likely when an ECG shows progressive PR prolongation followed by a dropped QRS complex?
questions.explanation: Mobitz type I, or Wenckebach, shows progressive PR interval lengthening until a QRS complex is dropped.
wrong: Mobitz type II has fixed PR intervals before dropped QRS complexes.
Third-degree AV block has complete AV dissociation.
Sinus arrest is a pause due to failure of sinus node impulse formation.
questions.takeaway: Progressive PR prolongation then drop = Mobitz I/Wenckebach.
